# The Long View — Episode
## What Sustainability Really Means (And Why Everyone Is Talking About It)
Host: Alex Morgan
Guest: Dr. Elena Fischer

[00:00]
Alex Morgan: Welcome to The Long View. Today we're exploring sustainability in depth with Dr. Elena Fischer, sustainability researcher and systems thinker.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Thanks, Alex Morgan. This is a topic I've spent decades on.

Alex Morgan: This episode is brought to you by Notion. Notion replaces the dozen tools your team is half-using with a single workspace for docs, projects, and wikis. Get started at notion.com/podcast.

[05:00]
Alex Morgan: Let's talk about systems thinking. What's your read?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Honestly, Sustainability is a systems-thinking problem before it's an environmental problem. Teams working with Carbon Trust run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Alex Morgan: Say more about that.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Most sustainability framing fails because it treats outcomes as targets instead of properties of systems. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.

[10:00]
Alex Morgan: Why is risk management the part that most analyses get wrong?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Let me give you the version I actually believe. The most expensive thing about climate is the optionality you lose by delaying. Teams working with Carbon Trust run into this constantly. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Alex Morgan: Where does that break down?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Reliability and sustainability share a vocabulary — failure modes, redundancy, and graceful degradation. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

[15:00]
Alex Morgan: Walk me through how risk management interacts with the broader sustainability story.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Yeah, so — Risk management at planetary scale looks like reliability engineering at company scale. Teams working with Carbon Trust run into this constantly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Alex Morgan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — I've changed my mind on individual action versus structural change. Both matter, but structural change moves the curve. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.

[20:00]
Alex Morgan: Walk me through how risk management interacts with the broader sustainability story.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Yeah, so — Most sustainability framing fails because it treats outcomes as targets instead of properties of systems. Teams working with IPCC run into this constantly. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Alex Morgan: That maps to what I've seen.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Carbon accounting needs the same rigor double-entry bookkeeping brought to finance. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.

[25:00]
Alex Morgan: A quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Visit linear.com/podcast for an extended trial.
Alex Morgan: Welcome back to the show.

[25:00]
Alex Morgan: Walk me through how risk management interacts with the broader sustainability story.
Dr. Elena Fischer: It comes down to this: Reliability and sustainability share a vocabulary — failure modes, redundancy, and graceful degradation. Teams working with IPCC run into this constantly. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Alex Morgan: Is there a counterexample that would change your view?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — The hardest part of climate work is staying technical when the conversation pulls toward identity. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.

[30:00]
Alex Morgan: Walk me through how risk management interacts with the broader sustainability story.
Dr. Elena Fischer: The short answer is I've changed my mind on individual action versus structural change. Both matter, but structural change moves the curve. Teams working with IPCC run into this constantly. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Alex Morgan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Sustainability is a systems-thinking problem before it's an environmental problem. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.

[35:00]
Alex Morgan: Let's talk about risk management. What's your read?
Dr. Elena Fischer: There are two angles — Carbon accounting needs the same rigor double-entry bookkeeping brought to finance. Teams working with Project Drawdown run into this constantly. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Alex Morgan: Where does that break down?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — The most expensive thing about climate is the optionality you lose by delaying. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.

[40:00]
Alex Morgan: What's the deepest piece of context on systems thinking that listeners are missing?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Honestly, The hardest part of climate work is staying technical when the conversation pulls toward identity. Teams working with Carbon Trust run into this constantly. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Alex Morgan: That tracks.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Risk management at planetary scale looks like reliability engineering at company scale. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.

[45:00]
Alex Morgan: What's the deepest piece of context on reliability that listeners are missing?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Sustainability is a systems-thinking problem before it's an environmental problem. Teams working with IPCC run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Alex Morgan: That tracks.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Most sustainability framing fails because it treats outcomes as targets instead of properties of systems. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.

[265:00]
Alex Morgan: What's the deepest piece of context on systems thinking that listeners are missing?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Honestly, Reliability and sustainability share a vocabulary — failure modes, redundancy, and graceful degradation. Teams working with Carbon Trust run into this constantly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Alex Morgan: That maps to what I've seen.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — The hardest part of climate work is staying technical when the conversation pulls toward identity. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.

[270:00]
Alex Morgan: Walk me through how reliability interacts with the broader sustainability story.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Yeah, so — I've changed my mind on individual action versus structural change. Both matter, but structural change moves the curve. Teams working with Project Drawdown run into this constantly. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Alex Morgan: Say more about that.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Sustainability is a systems-thinking problem before it's an environmental problem. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.

[275:00]
Alex Morgan: A quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Visit linear.com/podcast for an extended trial.
Alex Morgan: Welcome back to the show.

Alex Morgan: Why is systems thinking the part that most analyses get wrong?
Dr. Elena Fischer: There are two angles — Carbon accounting needs the same rigor double-entry bookkeeping brought to finance. Teams working with IPCC run into this constantly. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Alex Morgan: Where does that break down?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — The most expensive thing about climate is the optionality you lose by delaying. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.

Alex Morgan: Before we wrap, thanks again to our partners at Stripe. Stripe makes payments simple, whether you're running a marketplace or a subscription business.

Alex Morgan: Dr. Elena Fischer, thanks for the depth here.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Thank you, Alex Morgan.
Alex Morgan: That's The Long View — see you next time.
